reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Parental value on predictable, plannable admission processes and on fairness of access that does not punish families who cannot afford extra preparation
reactions.interest: Parents of children approaching selective-track admission age (roughly grade 4-6)
reactions.public_good_frame: A less coachable, more genuinely merit-based entry test broadens real opportunity for capable children regardless of family budget, which benefits the whole community's trust in the school system, not just our own children
reactions.argument: We want our children judged on ability rather than on how much test-prep we could afford, so a redesigned assessment is welcome in principle. But families plan years in advance around the current exam format — tutoring schedules, school visits, expectations set with the child. A redesign whose exact shape and coachability is untested (as the scenario itself flags) risks landing on our children as guinea pigs in the pilot cohorts, with the added stress of not knowing what 'the test' will actually look like when their turn comes.
reactions.condition_to_change: Full support if the new assessment and quota rules are announced with several years' lead time before any child is examined under them, and if pilot results are published so families can see how the format actually behaves before their own child sits it; opposition would follow rushed rollout with unclear or shifting rules close to a child's admission year

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Parental value on protecting children already enrolled from disruption (predictability) and on not increasing commuting burden or narrowing local school options for families still choosing
reactions.interest: Families with children currently enrolled in 6-/8-year tracks, and families with younger children who will face a shrinking set of selective places
reactions.public_good_frame: Protecting already-admitted pupils from having the rules change under them mid-schooling is a basic fairness owed to any child, and it lets families trust that a school placement decision, once made, will hold
reactions.argument: The felmenő rendszer guarantee for currently enrolled pupils is exactly what a parent wants to hear — our child's place won't be pulled out from under them. But the scenario itself flags real reversal risk if a future government drops the trajectory, and shrinking selective seats each year means families with younger children face a moving target: will there be a place at all, and will the 'advanced programme in general school' alternative actually exist and be nearby by the time it's needed, or will our child end up commuting further to whatever selective seats remain?
reactions.condition_to_change: Would support more strongly if the advanced-programme funding in general schools is visibly in place in our own district before the caps that would otherwise push us there start to bind, and if the entrenchment mechanism is credible enough that we're not planning around a policy that could vanish in the next election cycle

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Parental value on stable, predictable schooling through the middle-childhood years, on manageable and safe commuting for 10-14 year olds, and on avoiding mid-course disruption to a child's school arrangement
reactions.interest: Families with children currently aged roughly 8-14, who would fall inside the multi-year pilot-then-scale transition window
reactions.public_good_frame: Children's daily school routine, friendships, and commute are not abstractions that can be redrawn on a ministry timeline without cost to the individual child living through it, even where the long-run aim is fairer access for the whole cohort
reactions.argument: A restructuring that runs pilots for years and then scales nationally over roughly a decade means some children will start school under one structure and finish under another, or be moved into an untested 'comprehensive' pilot school while it is still finding its feet with newly retrained teachers. The scenario's own materials note there is no fallback plan if the anchoring political pact fails, and cite the Polish reversal as a live precedent — meaning a child could be moved through a network reconfiguration and then have it partially undone. Building or catchment reconfiguration also directly touches how far and how safely our children travel to school each day, and the scenario names 30-45 minute commuting tolerance as a real constraint, not a solved one.
reactions.condition_to_change: Would move toward support if the cross-party pact is secured and legally entrenched before any child is placed in a pilot or transition cohort, and if commuting-distance and safety impacts are assessed and capped per child before network changes are implemented; would harden into opposition if pilots proceed without that political anchoring, exposing real children to a reform that could be reversed mid-way

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Parental value on continuity of existing school choice and commuting arrangements, and on genuine (not merely announced) improvement for children in under-resourced schools
reactions.interest: All families with school-age children, both those in general schools and those using the existing selective track
reactions.public_good_frame: Keeping the structure families already understand and have planned their children's schooling around, while still directing real resources to weaker schools, serves both stability for today's children and better conditions for children in disadvantaged schools, without asking either group to bear disruption
reactions.argument: No change to selection age, track structure, or school networks means no new uncertainty for our children's day-to-day schooling, commute, or choice of school — this is the most predictable of the four options for a family currently navigating the system. The concern is that the scenario's own political-risk analysis flags the compensation funding as the easiest line to cut in a budget squeeze; if that happens, general-school families are left with the same under-resourced schools as before, having been told to expect improvement that didn't materialize, which is worse for those children's actual daily experience than never promising it.
reactions.condition_to_change: Would support fully if the compensation funding is placed on a multi-year, hard-to-cut budget footing so families in general schools can actually plan around promised improvements rather than hoping they survive the next budget cycle
